title: Tostones
subtitle: Twice-fried green plantain slices
excerpt: Green plantain slices fried until soft, flattened, then fried again until crisp and golden. The twice-fried technique is what distinguishes tostones from ordinary fried plantain, the second fry is what creates the crisp exterior and light interior.
sourceNotes: Tostones are twice-fried green plantain slices eaten across the Caribbean and Central America, in Cuba, Puerto Rico, the Dominican Republic, and Colombia. They are made from green (unripe) plantain, which is starchy and firm rather than sweet. True green plantain is not widely available in the UK; an unripe, all-green cooking banana is the closest substitute, though the texture is slightly denser. The key technique is the double fry: a first fry to soften and set the starch, then pressing the slices flat, then a second fry to achieve the crisp exterior. Tostones are a side dish or snack, served with garlic mojo, ketchup, or black beans.

content.text: The double fry is what makes tostones: the first fry softens the plantain and sets the starch; the flattening exposes a wider surface; the second fry crisps that surface to a crunch. A single fry produces something limp. The two-stage method is not optional.

Green plantain is not widely available in the UK. Use a fully green, unripe cooking banana. It has the same starchy quality. Do not use a ripe or yellow plantain or a dessert banana, which are sweet and will burn in the second fry rather than crisping.

items.name: Banana
items.prepNote: green, unripe cooking bananas, not ripe, not sweet

content.text: Peel the {{banana}} green bananas, score the skin lengthways with a knife and peel it away; the skin is tougher than a ripe banana and will not peel by hand. Cut each banana into rounds about 2.5 cm thick. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a deep frying pan over medium-high heat to about 175°C. Working in batches, fry the rounds for 3 minutes until pale gold and cooked through but not crisp. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper.

While still warm, press each round flat with the base of a glass, a mug, or a small plate, press firmly until the round is about 1 cm thick and roughly doubled in width. Return the flattened pieces to the hot oil in batches and fry for 2 to 3 minutes until deep golden and crisp all over. Remove and drain on fresh kitchen paper. Season immediately with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Serve straight away.

items.fix: The second fry needs oil at 175°C. Fry in small batches so the oil recovers temperature between batches. A crowded pan steams rather than fries.
items.cause: Oil not hot enough for the second fry; crowded pan lowering the oil temperature
items.symptom: Tostones soft and limp rather than crisp

items.fix: The first fry should leave the rounds just cooked through, pale gold, not fully golden. Flatten while still warm and pliable. Cut rounds 2.5 cm thick, not thinner.
items.cause: First fry too long; rounds too thick
items.symptom: Banana rounds falling apart when flattened

items.fix: Green, unripe banana is required. A yellow banana has too much sugar and will burn in the second fry. Keep oil at 175°C, higher temperatures scorch the exterior before the inside heats through.
items.cause: Ripe banana used; oil too hot

items.fix: Score the skin lengthways with a knife along the natural ridges before peeling. Green banana skin does not pull off by hand.

content.text: Tostones are one of the most common things on the table across the Spanish-speaking Caribbean and Central America. In Cuba they are a standard side with rice and beans. In Puerto Rico they show up with almost everything. In the Dominican Republic and Colombia they are street food. They take their name from the old Spanish coin (tostón) because of their flat round shape after pressing. In Jamaica and the English-speaking Caribbean cooks prefer the ripe sweet plantain (maduros, the yellow version). The twice-fried green version is the staple of the Hispanic Caribbean table.
